data.content:
The bug is clear — `ProcessPayment` only checks for zero, not negatives (line 29). The fastest workflow is `dotnet watch test`, which auto-reruns tests on every file save — no manual rebuild needed.

Let me start it and fix the bug:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The bug is clear: `ProcessPayment` only checks for `amount == 0` but not for negative amounts. The test expects an `ArgumentException` for negative amounts.

The fastest workflow is `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically on file save. Let me set that up, then fix the bug.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-4a61ec26f1c14184829d0dd1e5885687 && dotnet watch test --filter \"ProcessPayment_NegativeAmount_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch-test"}

data.content: Good — watch mode is running and caught the issue. Missing `using System;` plus the negative validation. Let me fix both:
data.reasoningText: Missing `using System;`. Let me fix both issues — the missing using and the negative amount validation.

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");

data.content:
✅ **Tests succeeded!** `dotnet watch` detected the file change and re-ran automatically — no manual rebuild needed.

Here's what I did and the workflow:

**The fix** — changed `amount == 0` → `amount <= 0` so negative amounts also throw `ArgumentException`, and added `using System;`.

**The fast-iteration workflow:**
1. Run `dotnet watch test --filter "YourTestName"` — it stays running
2. Edit your code and save
3. Watch auto-rebuilds and re-runs the filtered test instantly
4. Repeat until green ✅

This skips the full rebuild/restore cycle on each iteration. Let me clean up the watcher:
The watcher is stopped. Your final file looks like this:
All three tests pass. The key takeaway: **`dotnet watch test`** is your best friend for fast iteration — it watches for file changes and auto-reruns, eliminating the manual build-test cycle entirely.
